comments.text: There is a reference that sheds some light on why this question might be very difficult, despite its harmless appearance. Namely, a paper by Noga Alon, Amir Shpilka, and Christopher Umans, titled "On Sunflowers and Matrix Multiplication", shows that this conjecture is not compatible with one in computer science asserting that multiplication of two $n×n$ matrices is possible in $n^{2+o(1)}$ time. Very strange, since both of them sound so plausible at first glance...

This note grew out of extensive AI-assisted exploration over many sessions, numbering in the hundreds. AI contributed to the exploration of ideas, the synthesis of connections across intermediate results, proofs, and parts of the exposition. The mathematical claims I chose to record here, their final organization, and responsibility for any remaining errors are mine. I have done my best to ensure that the Lean formalizations and proofs are correct. I’m posting a cleaned-up draft of a note on partial results for Erdős sunflower problem \#20. This is not a proof of the full exponential sunflower conjecture. Rather, the note isolates several exact reductions, solved model classes, and obstruction examples that seem worth separating from larger notes. The main rigorous results I would currently foreground are as follows. First, there is an exact reduction to terminal kernels: if \(M_{\ker}(n,k)\) denotes the maximum size of a \(k\)-sunflower-free terminal family with edge sizes at most \(n\), then\[ M_{\ker}(n,k) \le M(n,k) \le (k-1)^n M_{\ker}(n,k). \]Second, there is an exact trace recursion: for a support piece \(P\) of rank \(r\) in an \(m\)-uniform family \(\mathcal{G}\),\[ |\mathcal{G}| = \sum_{\tau \in \operatorname{Tr}_P^*(\mathcal{G})} |\mathcal{G} * \tau|, \]and if the trace family is itself \(k\)-sunflower-free, then\[ |\mathcal{G}| \le f(r,k)\,f(m-r,k). \]Third, several model classes are completely solved in this framework, including bounded-degree terminal kernels, degree-two kernels, and exact block products. Fourth, there are fixed-alphabet transversal-code results, including an exact two-moment characterization of admissible \(5\)-columns over \(\mathbb{F}_5\), the value\[ F_{5,5}(2) = 16, \]and an explicit construction showing\[ F_{4,4}(3) \ge 28 > 3^3. \]Fifth, there are explicit obstruction families, including a zero-sum slice with all proper marginals uniform and a balanced multislice obstruction to naive positive-density box extraction. From this, it seems that the exact preprocessing to terminal kernels is clean, several natural model classes are tractable, and some attractive inverse heuristics are genuinely false. What is missing is a global routing theorem forcing arbitrarily large terminal kernels either into one of the solved classes or into a profitable recursive decomposition. I would especially appreciate overlap checks and references, particularly if any of the reductions, block-product results, obstruction examples, or small code-model values are already known in essentially this form. I would also appreciate criticism of the proofs or statements, as well as any other comments. I also linked the GitHub/Lean companion repository. Formalization status varies by item and is summarized in the draft. https://drive.google.com/file/d/11K2WF5dQEP92Syl3VVogswfrLudURJCL/view?usp=sharing https://github.com/SamMausberg/lean-formalizations/tree/main/FormalConjectures/Problems/Erdos/E20 Note: In my quick posting of this, I noticed I need to re-verify that some of these theorems are not present in the literature, as this is a popular problem.
Thanks! I ran standard check which claimed 1 minor issue and assessed the contribution as plausibly new but modest.

While working on Problem #857 I found the infrastructure also covers the uniform setting. Base cases f(1,3)=2 through f(6,3)=19 are formally certified in Lean 4. The conjecture statement ErdosProblem20 is formally defined. The general bound remains open. Repo: https://github.com/SproutSeeds/sunflower-lean *EDIT* @Terrance Tao Thank you for asking, happy to disclose fully. The development was built collaboratively using intelligence systems, primarily OpenAI Codex (GPT-5.2, extra-high reasoning effort) via the Codex CLI, and Anthropic Claude (Opus 4.5 and 4.6) via the Claude CLI. When Codex 5.3 and Claude Opus 4.6 released on the same day, I migrated to both gradually over the following 24-48 hours and continued the work on the newer models. Regarding the Aristotle references you noticed: early in the project I made one call to Aristotle to understand generally how it would lay out a Lean formalization. After seeing the general format, I took that as a starting point for the example files and then built everything else using Codex and Claude instead of the Aristotle API. The Aristotle files in the repo are those initial examples and nothing more. All substantive development was done with Codex and Claude. Codex and Claude are web services and the models themselves are opaque. What was not opaque was the process around them. I operated a structured orchestration process manually throughout, with explicit theorem targets, explicit acceptance criteria, and explicit rollback on failure. All building and verification was done locally, running 'lake build' on my own machine rather than relying on any cloud-based verification service. This kept the iteration loop tight: generate a proof attempt, build locally, accept or reject within seconds, move to the next target. That local feedback cycle was a significant part of what made the monotonic discipline practical to enforce in real time. The discovery process itself was also collaborative. Rather than arriving at theorem targets in isolation, I worked with the intelligence systems in an exploratory research mode first: discussing the mathematical landscape, identifying which lemmas were likely load-bearing, and understanding what the proof tree needed structurally before any Lean was written. The intelligence system would surface candidate directions, and I would evaluate them by asking more questions, to other intelligence systems and to myself. I am not separate from this process. I think of myself as a question-asking machine that happens to have good intelligence systems around it, always absorbing, always narrowing. The evaluation was not me sitting apart from the system doing mathematics independently. It was me as part of the system, using questions as the mechanism for narrowing toward a target. This adaptive layer is what guided the sequencing of proof passes. It did not generate results blindly. It nudged the direction of exploration based on what was already proved, what gaps remained, and what the most tractable next step looked like given the current state of the development. The core discipline was a monotonic sorry-free machine: every commit was only accepted if 'lake build' passed with zero sorries. If a proof attempt introduced a sorry, the commit was rejected and rolled back. This meant the proof tree could only grow and never regress. No sorry was ever allowed to persist across a commit boundary. The process worked like this: I would give the intelligence system a specific theorem target, it would produce a proof attempt, I would run the Lean kernel to verify it, and only accept it if it compiled sorry-free. The Lean kernel itself is completely independent of any intelligence system. It is the ground truth. The intelligence system generated candidates; the kernel decided validity. This separation was intentional and is the reason I trust the results. I chose Lean specifically as the backbone to keep my system honest. Over time the manual orchestration process evolved into a full automated orchestration system, a multi-agent proof search engine with persistent sessions, a theorem ledger, and the same monotonic sorry-free discipline enforced automatically. That system is now built and could push this work significantly further. The current bottleneck is purely compute. Token costs through the Claude and Codex APIs limit how much I can afford to run it. The SAT+LRAT certificates for M(5,3), M(6,3), and M(7,3) are fully reproducible independently. The LRAT files are in the repo and can be re-checked by any independent LRAT verifier without any involvement from an intelligence system. I am glad to add a disclosure statement to the repo README.

It appears that your Lean code is at least partially AI-generated (I see references to Aristotle, for instance). As per the policy of this site, could you edit your comment to disclose the nature of the AI use? (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

ALWZ, Rao, and Stoeckl successfully proved the upper bound limitation of $f(n,k) < (C_k \log n)^n$. However, as observed by Stoeckl (2022), their entropic mathematics are fundamentally reliant on creating $R$-spread randomized subsets using iterative regularization loops. The lingering open question has been whether the $\log n$ penalty is an inherent physical constant of maximal sunflower-free structures, or merely a byproduct of this spreadness-forcing regularization. Our new computational data strongly points to the latter. Focusing strictly on the Erdős $k=3$ regime, we bypassed spreadness-regularization entirely. We designed a bitmask-accelerated Simulated Annealing solver to construct deterministic, maximally dense $k=3$ sunflower-free families for dimensions $n \in \{3,4,5,6\}$. Rather than theoretically forcing spreadness to satisfy entropy inequalities, we computationally extracted the true empirical $\kappa$-spreadness thresholds directly from these physically optimal constructions. The data reveals a stark decoupling from the theoretical limits. As $n$ increases, the required normalized spreadness constant $C = \frac{\kappa}{\log_2 n}$ to avoid forming a 3-sunflower is strictly decaying ($2.44 \to 1.99 \to 1.23 \to 0.96$). The log n factor appears artificial. Evidence suggests f(n,3) < Cⁿ with C ≈ 2-3, potentially eliminating the logarithmic factor entirely. If the logarithmic penalty was physically required to build dense extremal families, the required $\kappa$ threshold would scale linearly alongside $\log n$. It decidedly does not. This topological structural decay isolates the $\log n$ parameter directly to the regularization loops used in current Sieve methodology, verifying that the current $(C \log n)^n$ bounds are mathematically artificial limits. This exact computational data provides robust experimental validity to Erdős’s original instinct: the true maximal limit behaves squarely as $C^n$. Mr. @TerenceTao We respectfully request your expert verification of: 1. Whether our f(4,3) ≥ 36 and f(5,3) ≥ 60 constructions represent legitimate improvements to known bounds. 2. If our spreadness decay evidence provides sufficient reason to reconsider the log n factor in theoretical descriptions. 3. Whether these computational results, if verified, warrant updating Erdős Problem #20 Our deterministic arrays, the combinatorial SA engine, and the exact mathematical spreadness metrics are open-sourced for peer evaluation here:https://github.com/rynald0cst0ltziam/erdos-sunflower-decay and our manuscript here https://github.com/rynald0cst0ltziam/erdos-sunflower-decay/blob/main/MANUSCRIPT.md

For context, existing lower bounds for fixed k, in particular k = 3, already grow like cⁿ for some c > 1, so a bound of the form f(n,k) ≤ (cₖ)ⁿ would not contradict known constructions. What still seems genuinely open is whether the current (log n)ⁿ overhead in the best upper bounds is inherent or just an artifact of the iterative regularization used to force spreadness. In the ALWZ framework and its refinements, the log n appears to come from repeatedly restricting the family until it becomes spread, rather than from the sunflower-extraction step itself. It would be very interesting to know whether the “popular witness” certifying non-spreadness can be exploited directly to build a sunflower, bypassing the regularization loop altogether. If there are post-ALWZ references, even in special classes of n-uniform families, that achieve a true (cₖ)ⁿ bound or that explain why such a bound cannot follow from spreadness-based methods alone, I would be grateful for pointers.

Lunjia Hu (here) improved the position of $C$ given in the current best bound of [BCW21], though slightly. Manuel Stoeckl (here) further refined the constant and gave this result: There exists a universal constant $C≤64$, so that for any $r≥1$, $k≥2$, every set system containing $≥(Cr \log k)^k$ sets of size $≤k$ contains an r-petaled sunflower. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

Like [826] and [469], a hypothetical solution would reduce of size of certain number-theoretic proofs. Whether this can actually be done in these situations, is a different story altogether. Perhaps this can help to explain why this problem is so difficult.
